title-group.article-title: Primary peripheral nerve repair: A systematic review of sutures, fibrin glue, and biologic-augmented approaches

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Pain Medicine, Mayo Clinic, Jacksonville, Florida, USA
bDepartment of Orthopedic Surgery, Mayo Clinic, Jacksonville, Florida, USA
cEvidence-Based Practice Center, Mayo Clinic, Rochester, Minnesota, USA
dRobert D. and Patricia E. Kern Center for the Science of Health Care Delivery, Mayo Clinic, Minnesota, USA
eDepartment of Anesthesiology, Mayo Clinic, Jacksonville, Florida, USA
fCenter for Regenerative Biotherapeutics, Mayo Clinic, Jacksonville, Florida, USA

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Fibrin glue neurorrhaphy (FGN) has demonstrated functional improvements for traumatic peripheral nerve injuries (PNI) in preclinical studies; however, evidence regarding its clinical effectiveness in humans remains limited. Recent advances in regenerative medicine, particularly involving biologics such as platelet-rich plasma (PRP), represent an innovative therapeutic strategy that leverages its neuroprotective, anti-inflammatory, and immunomodulatory effects as well as trophic support for tissue regeneration. To assess the safety and effectiveness of these approaches, we conducted a systematic review comparing FGN and microsuture neurorrhaphy (MN), used either individually or in combination with biologics, for primary peripheral nerve repair.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Systematic literature searches were conducted across multiple databases from 1990 to 2025 in accordance with PRISMA guidelines, and the protocol was registered with PROSPERO. The primary outcome of interest was motor and sensory functional recovery and safety. Other outcomes included neurophysiological examinations, pain or discomfort and operating time. The Revised Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool for Randomized Trials(RoB2) and Newcastle-Ottawa Scale(NOS) were used to assess the risk of bias.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 164 patients from six studies were included. The mean patient age was 31.43 years, and 61.6% were female. Compared with MN, FGN achieved comparable motor and sensory recovery while offering shorter operative times and simpler technical execution. When combined with PRP, faster functional recovery was observed, and no major complications were reported.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: FGN may represent a sutureless alternative to MN with comparable functional outcomes in selected patients for primary peripheral nerve repair. The adjunctive use of PRP may facilitate nerve regeneration and improve functional recovery without introducing major safety concerns. However, given the heterogeneity of nerve types and the high risk of bias among included studies, these findings should be interpreted with caution, and require confirmation in adequately powered future studies.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Microsuture neurorrhaphy (MN) is widely regarded as the gold standard for end-to-end (ETE) coaptation in peripheral nerve injuries. It is primarily used for short nerve defects, allowing for tension-free approximation and coaptation of damaged proximal and distal stumps.1, 2, 3

Recent advancements in the understanding of peripheral nerve pathophysiology along with refinements in MN techniques, have notably improved functional outcomes.4 However, the time-consuming and technically demanding nature of MN has driven the exploration of alternative coaptation strategies, particularly for complex nerve injuries or multiple nerve repairs.5, 6, 7, 8

Fibrin glue neurorrhaphy (FGN) offers a sutureless alternative to MN acting as hemostatic agents or biological adhesives for coaptation of peripheral nerves.9 Despite its promising potential to reduce operative time, simplify technical execution and minimize tissue manipulation, comparative evidence with conventional MN remains notably limited. Meanwhile, there is a growing scientific interest in combination therapeutic strategies that incorporate biologics such as platelet-rich plasma (PRP) as adjuvant interventions to augment nerve regeneration following MN and FGN due to its multifaceted immunomodulatory, anti-inflammatory, and healing-promoting properties.10,11 However, the current evidence is largely confined to preclinical studies, with limited translation into clinical practice.

This systematic review aims to comprehensively synthesize current clinical evidence on the safety and effectiveness of FGN compared with MN used either individually or in combination with biologics for primary peripheral nerve repair. We hypothesize that FGN and MN will yield comparable clinical outcomes with a shorter operative time for FGN, and that their combination with biologics is surgically feasible, safe, and may be associated with faster motor and sensory recovery.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The study was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-analyses (PRISMA) reporting guidelines.12 The review protocol was registered on the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO Registration Number: CRD42023397644). Patients or the public were not involved in the design, conduct, reporting, or dissemination plans of our research.

sec.title: Literature search strategy
sec.p: Systematic searches were performed in multiple electronic databases from 1990 to December 15, 2025, without language restrictions. The databases included Ovid MEDLINE(R) and Epub Ahead of Print, In-Process & Other Non-Indexed Citations, and Daily, Ovid EMBASE, Ovid Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, Ovid Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, and Scopus. The search strategy was developed and implemented by an experienced medical reference librarian with input from study investigators. Controlled vocabulary supplemented with keywords was used to search for platelet-rich plasma or fibrin gel therapy studies for adult patients with traumatic nerve injury. The actual search strategy is available in Supplementary Appendix A1. In addition, a manual literature search was performed to supplement the capture of studies.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: We included all published RCTs, comparative observational studies, and case series that assessed the safety or effectiveness of fibrin gel containing any types of biologically active substances in adult patients for the treatment of primary PNIs. Biologics include any types of platelet-rich plasma (PRP), platelet lysate (PL) or platelet-rich growth factor (PRGF) with or without a carrier, growth factors (GF), mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), or extracellular vesicles (EVs). Animal studies and nerve grafting were excluded. Studies were also excluded if they did not present original data (e.g., clinical reviews, editorials, letters, or erratum). Detailed inclusion and exclusion criteria can be found in Supplementary Table A.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: Independent reviewers, operating in pairs, screened the titles and abstracts of all citations identified through the systematic literature search using Covidence systematic review software (Veritas Health Innovation, Melbourne, Australia). Subsequently, potentially eligible citations were retrieved for full-text screening by independent reviewers working in pairs for inclusion. Any disagreements were resolved through discussion with a senior reviewer.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: A standardized data extraction form was developed and piloted to capture study details. Independent reviewers extracted the following information:1) study characteristics (authors and year, country(s), study design, study funding, treatment groups, number of participants, age and gender, nerve type, quantity of biologics/FG applied, concurrent therapy and length of follow-up); 2) Outcome measures including motor and sensory recovery, safety/adverse events, operative time, pain/discomfort/satisfaction, and neurophysiological assessments.

sec.title: Outcomes of interest
sec.p: The primary outcome of interest is motor and sensory functional recovery and safety. Other outcomes of interest include neurophysiological examinations, discomfort and operative time.

sec.title: Assessment of risk of bias
sec.p: Two independent review authors assessed risk bias, with discrepancies adjudicated by a third reviewer. For RCTs, the RoB 2 was utilized to evaluate bias related to randomization processes, deviations from interventions, incomplete outcome data, measurement of outcomes, and selection of reported results.13 For NRCTs, the NOS was employed to evaluate the representativeness of the study population, comparability of the groups, and ascertainment of the outcome of interest. A follow-up period of 12 months or longer was deemed acceptable for outcomes to occur, and a follow-up rate greater than 80% was considered adequate.14

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p: Of 776 distinct citations, six studies involving 164 unique patients met the eligibility criteria. The average age of the participants was 31.43(15–65), and 61.6% were female (n = 101).

The selection process and exclusion reasons are illustrated in the PRISMA flowchart, Figure 1. Among the included studies, three randomized controlled trials (RCTs)15, 16, 17 and one nonrandomized controlled trial (NRCTs)18 compared the outcomes of FGN versus MN, and two NRCTs19,20 evaluated the effectiveness of MN or FGN in combination of PRP. These studies were conducted in four countries: three in Asia (two in India, and one in Japan), two in Africa (two in Egypt) and one in North America (USA).Figure 1
PRISMA flow diagram for study selection and screening process.Figure 1 dummy alt text

The treatment groups included: (1) MN only (n = 67), (2) FGN only (n = 77), (3) MN with PRP (n = 6), and (4) FGN with PRP (n = 14). FGN was prepared autologously in one study15 and commercially in three studies.16,18 Among the included studies, MN was performed using 8–0, 9–0, or 10–0 sutures in four studies15,16,18 and one study20 did not specify the suture size. The role of PRP as an adjunct to MN and FGN was explored in two NRCTs.19,20 The average follow-up was 18.05 months (range: 6–144 months). Study characteristics are presented in Table 1.Table 1
Summary of study characteristics.Table 1 dummy alt text
Publication Author, YearCountryStudy typeStudy fundingTreatment group (Treatment; Control)No. of patients (Treatment; Control)Age, mean ± SD/range (Years); Female, N (%)Nerve typeVolume applied (FG/PRP)Concurrent therapyLength of follow-up
Sallam et al.,15EgyptRCT, UnblindedNRAutologous FGN; MN52(27; 25)FGN: 36.5(20–58); 14 (32.5); MN:35.9 (21–58); 15 (35.7%)Ulnar, median, or both nervesA few drops for sufficient coverage; coaptation site3 wks post-op: passive stretching; 6 wks post-op: electrical stimulation (5 days per wk for 6 wks; High-frequency (30 Hz) alternating current till voluntary muscle contraction), Self-administered instructionsMinimal 1 Y; Mean F/U: 15.2 M(FGN); 14.5 M(MN)
Abulezz et al.,16EgyptRCT, UnblindedNRFGN; MN16(10; 6)FGN:20.5; 3(20%);MN:21.4; 2(13.3%)Median, ulnar, or radial nervesA couple of drops at coaptation siteA plaster cast was maintained for 4 wks post-op, followed by a comprehensive physical therapy regimen lasting two years.2 Y; Mean F/U:18 M
Haq et al.,17IndiaRCT, single-blindNoneFGN; MN50(25; 25)FGN: 31.0 ± 11.62; 20(80); MN:30.8 ± 12.23; 18(72)Median, ulnar, or both nervesNRNo2 Y; Mean F/U:18 M
Kanzaki et al.,18JapanNRCTNRFGN; MN6(1; 5)50.5(44–56); 4(66.7%)Intracranial facial nerveNRThree patients from MN underwent hypoglossal-facial nerve anastomosis 1.3 to 1.5 years later due to poor recovery of facial function.Mean F/U: 60 M(12 M-144 M)
Serratelli et al.,19USANRCTNoneFGN + autologous PRP; FGN Only28(14; 14)26.6 (15–52); 21(75%)Lingual nerve3 mL; PRP gel placed over the nerve repair siteNoMean F/U: 8.07 ± 3.36 M(FGN + autologous PRP); 10.57 ± 3.77 M(FGN Only)
Das et al.,20IndiaNRCTNil; No COIMN + autologous PRP; MN Only12(6; 6)MN + autologous PRP: 29.33; 2 (33.3%); MN Only: 28.33, 2 (33.3%)Median, ulnar nervesAround 0.4–0.5 mL; 22 G needle; Injection into epineurium and around injured nerveNo12 M
COI, conflict of interest; F/U, follow-up; FG, fibrin glue; FGN, fibrin glue neurorrhaphy; M, month(s); mL, milliliter; MN, microsuture neurorrhaphy; N/No., Number; NR, not reported; NRCT, non-randomized controlled trial; Post-op, postoperatively; PRP, platelet rich plasma; RCT, randomized controlled trial; SD, standard deviation; uL, microliter; wks, week(s); Y, Year(s).

All six studies performed ETE primary nerve repair. Nerve grafting was beyond the scope of this review and was therefore excluded. Three RCTs reported the average duration between injury and surgery,15,16 ranging from 6.4 days to 4.91 months. Two RCT15 and one NRCT20 performed internal or external neurolysis before the nerve repair. In studies19,20 using the combination approach, MN or FGN was performed first followed by PRP administration to the coaptation site, either in gel form or injected within the epineurium. Two RCTs15,16 implemented concurrent rehabilitation including passive stretching exercises, electrical stimulation, self-directed home exercises/physical therapy. The product formulation and surgical methodologies are depicted in Supplementary Table B.

sec.title: Motor and sensory recovery
sec.p: Three RCTs15, 16, 17 demonstrated no significant differences in functional outcomes between FGN and MN for the repair of ulnar, median, or both nerve injuries at final follow-up, as assessed by the British Medical Research Council Scale (BMRCS), Highet’s Scheme Classification (HSCS), Two Point Discrimination(2TD), Semmes-Weinstein Monofilament test (SW) or Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder, and Hand questionnaire(DASH). Similarly, comparable outcomes were observed between the FGN and MN groups in a NRCT of intracranial facial nerve repair.18

In an NRCT19 using FGN in combination of PRP for facial nerve repair, no statistically significant differences were observed between groups in von Frey filament testing (FGN + PRP: 2.43 ± 1.09; FGN Only: 2.00 ± 0.00; p = 0.274) or 2PD (FGN + PRP: 10.93 ± 3.43 mm; FGN Only: 9.79 ± 3.29 mm; p = 0.326). Facial sensory recovery assessed using the BMRCS showed the time to achieve S3+ sensory recovery was significantly shorter in the FGN + PRP group (5.5 ± 1.4 months) compared with the FGN Only group (9.5 ± 4.16 months; p = 0.008).

In another NRCT20 evaluating the combination approach of MN with PRP for median or ulnar nerve repair, muscle strength assessed by the BMRCS in muscle strength (M0-M5) improved progressively in both groups over time, with significantly greater recovery in the MN + PRP group at 3 months (p = 0.014), 6 months (p = 0.013), and 12 months (p = 0.002). Tactile spatial acuity assessed by static and dynamic 2PD (S2PD and D2PD) also improved significantly in the MN + PRP group with a greater recovery at 12 months in S2PD (p = 0.043) and 6 months in D2PD (p = 0.036). For the sensory recovery, BMRCS (S0-S5) showed faster and greater improvement in the MN + PRP group compared with the MN Only group at 12 months (p = 0.046). In addition, the QuickDASH questionnaire showed significantly greater improvement in upper-limb function in the MN + PRP group compared with the MN-only group at 12 months (p < 0.001).

sec.title: Operative time, adverse events and pain/discomfort
sec.p: Among four studies,15, 16, 17, 18 FGN was consistently reported to be technically easier and less time-consuming than MN. One RCT15 reported a shorter mean operative time in the FGN group compared with the MN group (FGN: 6.8 min; range, 6–8 min; MN: 22 min, range, 20–26 min). Similarly, another RCT17 demonstrated a significantly shorter operative time for FGN than for MN (FGN: 9.92 ± 6.57 min; MN: 21.04 ± 8.06 min; p < 0.05).

No infection or wound dehiscence was observed in Serratelli et al.’s study using FG combined with PRP.19 The authors also notes that FGN+ PRP improved healing, reduced wound dehiscence, and minimized patient discomfort compared to the FGN Only group. However, no objective outcome instruments were used. Another NRCT15 on intracranial facial nerve reconstruction reported difficulties with eating and speaking, eye irritation, and reduced tear secretion in the FGN group, all of which gradually resolved as facial nerve function improved.18 Sallam et al. reported superficial wound infection and fixed deformities were in both MN and FGN groups.

sec.title: Neurophysiologic evaluation
sec.p:
Electromyography was used in a RCT where comparable median or ulnar nerve recovery was observed at the 1-year follow-up between the FGN and MN groups including similar median amplitude, latency, and duration of the compound motor unit action potential.15 In an NRCT, electroneurography showed significantly greater motor (p = 0.003) and sensory (p = 0.004) nerve conduction velocities in the MN + PRP group compared with the MN Only group.20 Further outcome details are provided in Table 2.Table 2
Outcomes of functional recovery, operative time, pain/discomfort, neurophysiological examination.Table 2: dummy alt text
Publication author, yearMotor/Sensory recoveryOperative time mean ± SD/ Mean(time range)Neurophysiological examinationComplications/Discomfort
Sallam et al.,15BMRCSc (muscle strength, a grade of M3 or more): 1) FGN, n = 21(72.4%); 2) MN, n = 20(80%); p = 0.52.BMRCSe (sensory recovery, a grade of S3 or more): 1) FGN, n = 17(65.4%); 2) MN, n = 19(79.2%); p = 0.28.1) FGN: 6.8(6–8) mins; 2) MN: 22(20–26) minsAt 1 Y, the Median(range) of amplitude(mV), latency(mS), and duration(mS) of the cMUAP of the median nerve and ulnar nerve were reported. 1) EMGR: Median nerve, n = 24: 4(3–5), 5(4–6), 8(8–12); Ulnar nerve n = 30: 4(3–5), 5(4–7), 9(8–12); 2) MRC: Median nerve n = 24: 4(3–6), 4.5(4–7), 9(8–12); Ulnar nerve n = 30: 3.75(3–5), 5(4–7), 9(8–12).In the FGN group, 2 pts experienced superficial wound infections, and 4 pts developed fixed deformities; In the MN group, 2 pts encountered superficial wound infections, and 6 pts presented with fixed deformities.
Abulezz et al.,16BMRCSc (muscle strength, M2 or 3 = Fair; M4 or 5 = Good): 1) FGN, n = 15(100%): Good, n = 10(66.7%); Fair, n = 5(33.3%); 2) MN, n = 14(93.3%): Good, n = 10(66.7%); Fair, n = 4(26.7%); Bad, n = 1(6.7%).HSCSf:1) FGN, n = 15(100%): Good, n = 10(80%); Fair, n = 3(20%); 2) MN, n = 15(100%): Good, n = 10(66.7%); Fair, n = 5(33.7%).FGN significantly reduces operation time, particularly in cases involving multiple technically challenging cable grafts.
Haq et al.,172PDa: No. of patients in categories 1–4. 1) FGN, n = 25(100%): 1, 3, 14, 7; 2) MN, n = 25(100%): 1, 2, 14, 8; p > 0.05.SWb: No. of patients in categories 1–5. 1) FGN, n = 25(100%): 0, 2, 5, 13, 5; 2) MN, n = 25(100%): 0, 3, 6, 10, 6; p > 0.05.BMRCSc (motor recovery, a grade of M3 or more): 1) FGN, n = 21(84%); 2) MN, n = 21(84%); p > 0.05.DASH questionnaired: 1) FGN, Mean ± SD: 22.88 ± 14.89; 2) MN: Mean ± SD: 25.04 ± 15.65; p > 0.05.1) FGN, 9.92 ± 6.57 mins; 2) MN: 21.04 ± 8.06 mins; p < 0.05.
Kanzaki et al.,18Yanagihara’s ratingg: 1) FGN, n = 1: 20; 2) MN, n = 5: 17.2.FGN approach was technically easy to perform and less time-consuming than MN.Difficulties with eating, speaking, eye Irritation, and reduced tear secretion were observed in the early stages, which gradually resolved as facial function improved.
Serratelli et al.,19von Frey filament: FGN + PRP: 2.43 ± 1.09; FGN Only: 2.0 ± 0; p = 0.274.2PD: FGN + PRP: 10.93 mm ± 3.43 mm; FGN Only: 9.79 mm ± 3.29 mm; p = 0.326.BMRCSe (sensory recovery): FGN + PRP: n = 1(S2+), n = 5(S3), n = 8(S3+), mean FSR:3.43 ± 0.646; FGN Only: n = 1(S2+), n = 3(S3), n = 10(S3+), mean FSR:3.64 ± 0.633; p = 0.384.Time achieved S3+in BMRCS: FGN + PRP: 5.5 M ± 1.4 M; FGN Only: 9.5 M ± 4.16 M; p = 0.008.No infections or wound dehiscence were observed.PRP use improved soft tissue healing, reduced wound dehiscence, and decreased patient discomfort.
Das et al.,20BMRCSh (muscle strength): Pts in Categories (M0-M5)/Summed Score (0–10). 1) At 1M, MN + PRP: 6, 0, 0, 0; MN Only: 6, 0, 0, 0; 2) At 3 M, MN + PRP: 0, 6, 0, 0; MN Only: 4, 2, 0, 0; p = 0.014; 3) At 6 M, MN + PRP: 0, 1, 5, 0; MN Only: 1, 5, 0, 0; p = 0.013; 4) At 12 M, MN + PRP: 0, 0, 0, 6; MN Only: 0, 2, 4, 0; p = 0.002.S2DT and D2DTa: No. of Pts in Categories 1–4. S2DT: 1) At 1 M, MN + PRP: 6, 0, 0, 0; MN Only: 6, 0, 0, 0; 2) At 3 M, MN + PRP: 6, 0, 0, 0; MN Only: 6, 0, 0, 0; 3) At 6 M, MN + PRP: 2, 3, 1, 0; MN Only: 5, 1, 0, 0; p = 0.013; 4) At 12 M, MN + PRP: 0, 0, 5, 1; MN Only: 0, 4, 2, 0; p = 0.043. D2DT: 1) At 1 M, MN + PRP: 6, 0, 0, 0; MN Only: 6, 0, 0, 0; 2) At 3 M, MN + PRP: 6, 0, 0, 0; MN Only: 6, 0, 0, 0; 3) At 6 M, MN + PRP: 1, 1, 4, 0; MN Only: 5, 1, 0, 0; p = 0.036; 4) At 12 M, MN + PRP: 0, 0, 3, 3; MN Only: 0, 4, 2, 0; p = 0.076.BMRCS (sensory recovery)e: No. of Pts in categories S0-S4. BMRCS: 1) At 1 M, MN + PRP: 6, 0, 0, 0, 0; MN Only: 6, 0, 0, 0, 0; 2) At 3 M, MN + PRP: 1, 2, 3, 0, 0; MN Only: 0, 5, 1, 0, 0; p = 0.193; 3) At 6 M, MN + PRP: 0, 0, 2, 4, 0; MN Only: 0, 0, 4, 2, 0; p = 0.248; 4) At 12 M, MN + PRP: 0, 0, 0, 3, 3; MN Only: 0, 0, 0, 6, 0; p = 0.046.QuickDASH questionnaired: 1) MN + PRP, Mean ± SD: 30.83 ± 9.925; 2) MN Only: Mean ± SD: 22.33 ± 3.266; p < 0.001.At 6 M, 1) ENG (motor velocity aminths (m/s), Mean ± SD): MN + PRP: 41.938 ± 1.734; MN Only: 36.770 ± 2.564; p = 0.003; 2) NCS (sensory velocity(m/s), Mean ± SD): MN + PRP: 40.827 ± 1.404; MN Only: 35.027 ± 3.104; p = 0.004.
BMRCS, British Medical Research Council Scale; DASH, Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder, and Hand questionnaire; D/S 2PD, Dynamic/Static Two Point Discrimination; EMG, electromyography; ENG, electroneurography; F/U, follow-up; FGN, fibrin glue neurorrhaphy; HSCS, Highet’s Scheme Classification System; M, month(s); Mins, minute(s); MR, microsuture neurorrhaphy; mS, millisecond(s); mV, millivoltage(s); NCS, nerve conduction study; No., Number; NR, not reported; Post-op, postoperatively; Pts, patient(s); SD, standard deviation; SW, Semmes-Weinstein Monofilament test; Y, year(s).a
S2DT and D2DT outcomes in 4 categories:1 = >15 mm (which is the worst score), 2 = 11–15 mm, 3 = 6–10 mm, 4 = 1–5 mm (which is the best score).b
SW(sensory recovery) outcomes in 6 categories: 0 = not testable, 1 = filament 6.65, 2 = filament 4.56(4 g), 3 = filament 4.31(2,0 g), 4 = filament 3.61(0.2 g), 5 = filament 2.83(0.05 g).c
BMRCS (motor recovery) in six grades, from M0 to M5. M0 or M1 = Bad; M2 or M3 = Fair; M4 or M5 = Good. Normal as the control scoring M5. Good and Fair considered as functional recovery.d
QuickDASH/DASH questionnaire: higher scores indicate greater impairment in upper-limb function.e
BMRCS (Sensory recovery): S0, S1/S1+, S2/S2+, S3/S3+, and S4.f
HSCS: S0-S4, S4 or S3+ = Good, S3 or S2+ = Fair, S2, S1, S1+ or S0 = Bad. Good and Fair considered as functional recovery.g
Yanagihara’s rating for facial nerve function (full score of 40 points). The higher score, the better recovery.h
BMRCS(muscle strength): M0-M5/Summed Score (0–10): the scores of two muscles supplied by the particular injured nerve were summed to derive a score ranging from 0 to 10, and the scores were grouped as 0, 1–3, 4–6, and 7–10. The higher score, the better recovery.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p: Two unblinded RCTs15,16 were rated as having a high risk of bias in the Measurement of Outcomes domain due to an open-label study design, resulting in an overall high risk of bias. The remaining single-blind RCT17 was rated overall as having some concerns because of the insufficient detail in the Randomization process and Measurement of Outcomes domains. All three NRCTs were rated as having a low risk of bias in the “Selection” but a high risk in the Comparability domain due to the absence of appropriate control groups for confounders, resulting in an overall low risk of bias in two studies18,20 and moderate risk in one study,19 Supplementary Table C1-C2.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review comprehensively synthesizes the current clinical evidence on the safety and effectiveness of FGN and MN for primary peripheral nerve repair, performed either individually or in combination with platelet-rich plasma. Comparative motor and sensory outcomes were observed between FGN and MN. When both FGN and MN repair are viable options, FGN may offer several practical advantages such as a shorter operative time, and greater technical simplicity.15,17 These clinical findings are consistent with two systematic reviews and meta-analyses in preclinical settings, which also demonstrated similar motor and sensory outcomes between FGN and MN, with FGN reporting operative times approximately three to four times shorter than MN.21,22

Safety and complication profiles are key considerations in treatment selection. In this review, Sallam et al. documented postoperative complications in both the FGN (n = 6) and MN (n = 8) groups following ulnar, median, or combined nerve repairs, including superficial wound infections and fixed deformities secondary to soft tissue contracture. Kanzaki et al. reported early postoperative challenges such as difficulties in eating and speaking, ocular irritation, and reduced lacrimation which resolved with progressive facial recovery. Taken together, these observations suggest a potential risk of allergic reactions or adverse responses in individuals with hypersensitivity. Prior literature including several case reports have also suggested that specific fibrin glue constituents, such as aprotinin and certain suture materials, may be associated with allergic adverse effects.23, 24, 25, 26, 27 One study reported an estimated incidence of 0.5 per 100,000 fibrin glue applications.21 In this review, the use of FGN or MN, either alone or in combination with PRP, did not differ in terms of infections and was generally reported to be safe across the reviewed studies. It is worth noting that the type of nerve involved should be considered for dishesence as the loss of tensile strength in fibrin glue represents a major confounding factor. The facial region is characterized by limited motion and lower mechanical stress, whereas limb repairs are exposed to greater tension and a higher risk of coaptation failure.

The challenge with nerve reconstruction, whether primary or delayed, lies in the progressive deterioration of the target end organs prior to reinnervation of the motor endplates. In clinical practice, nerve reconstruction is often delayed up to 6 months to allow potential spontaneous recovery of the host nerve. However, degeneration of motor endplates typically begins around 1 year following the initial traumatic insult. Given the intrinsic axonal regeneration rate of approximately 1–3 mm per day, the likelihood of achieving meaningful functional recovery after brachial plexus injury becomes increasingly unpredictable when repair is attempted beyond 6 months post-injury. From a technical perspective, incorporating biologically active substances into FG or using FGN alone at the neurorrhaphy site appears to confer limited additional benefit. FGN simplifies the procedure and shortens operative time relative to conventional small caliber MN approach but potential denaturation of bioactive substance within FG formulations requires further evaluation as it may diminish therapeutic potential.21,22 In general, the use of fibrin glue alone is considered appropriate when there are ample nerve redundancy and minimal risk of tension at the coaptation site. In contrast, a combination of suture repair and fibrin glue is typically employed when there is concern regarding the stability or potential failure of the coaptation with FGN alone. This fundamental surgical consideration appears to have been overlooked in the discussion of the included studies. It is important to note that MN does not inherently improve fascicular alignment but rather serves to reinforce the mechanical integrity of the repair. FGN may serve as a useful adjunct or alternative in selected clinical scenarios, such as short nerve gaps, fragile tissues, or time-sensitive procedures. However, it cannot yet be considered a complete substitute for conventional suture repair. In this context, combining FGN with a couple positional sutures may offer a balanced strategy that preserves technical efficiency while ensuring accurate fascicular orientation.

Fibrin glue and microsuturing primarily function as coaptation agents, securing nerve ends and facilitating axonal regeneration following nerve injury.28,29 These materials are typically biocompatible and bioabsorbable, providing temporary mechanical support; however, their therapeutic effects may be impeded by scar tissue infiltration, which disrupts the favorable microenvironment required for optimal nerve healing and regeneration. The incorporation of biologics, including PRP and other growth factors, has attracted increasing interest due to their potential to modulate this microenvironment. In this review, two comparative studies explored such combination therapeutic strategies (FGN with PRP and MN with PRP) demonstrating a favorable safety profile and faster functional recovery in primary facial, median, and ulnar nerve repairs.19,20 The proposed multifaceted immunomodulatory, anti-inflammatory and healing-promoting mechanisms of PRP including secretion of anti-inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-10, TGF-β, IL-4, IL-13),30, 31, 32, 33 insulin-like growth factor-1-mediated neuroprotection and neurotrophic factor-driven axonal regeneration34, 35, 36 are predominantly supported by preclinical evidence and remain to be confirmed in clinical studies. A similar combination approach using MN with fibrin glue supplemented with acidic fibroblast growth factor (aFGF) has also been reported for brachial plexus and common peroneal nerve injuries in selected nerve grafting cases, showing improved axonal regeneration and functional recovery compared with MN alone.37 Other biologics such as mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) and MSC-derived extracellular vesicles (EVs) are emerging where they have been combined with FGN or MN to promote nerve regeneration, enhance functional recovery, and alleviate neuropathic pain following peripheral nerve injury.38, 39, 40, 41, 42, 43, 44 However, its clinical evidence is still lacking, although these findings provide promising insights to guide future clinical applications in peripheral nerve repair.

Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) is the most commonly used biologic in the included studies; however, preparation methods vary widely across studies and clinical settings, not only among the studies included in this review but also across other therapeutic areas. It is typically prepared through differential centrifugation of anticoagulated blood but can differ significantly by centrifugation type and speed, activation method, anticoagulant used, and baseline platelet count influencing yield.45,46 Standardization of these manufacturing parameters will be critical to ensure the safety, effectiveness, and reproducibility of this combination treatment strategy. Multiple systematic reviews have recommended developing consensus guidelines and standardized reporting frameworks to harmonize clinical research and practice.47, 48, 49, 50 For future investigations of combination strategies for peripheral nerve injuries, the establishment of a standardized PRP preparation protocol should be a priority. Also, comprehensive characterization and transparent reporting of the final PRP product would greatly improve study reproducibility and strengthen its clinical applicability.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This study has several limitations. First, despite a comprehensive literature search, we identified six eligible studies comprising 164 patients. The small number of studies and limited sample size have reduced the statistical power to detect true differences between FGN, MN and biologic-augmented approaches. The unblinded design of the RCTs introduces potential measurement bias, and the NRCTs were susceptible to comparability bias due to uncontrolled confounders. Second, the comparative groups were nonparallel across studies; some evaluated FGN or MN alone, whereas others assessed combinations of MN or FGN with adjunctive PRP. Moreover, substantial clinical and methodological heterogeneity were present, including inconsistent outcome measures and postoperative regimens which further complicates the evaluation of effectiveness. Together, these factors complicate the interpretation of pooled results and limit the ability to draw definitive conclusions regarding the relative effectiveness of each nerve repair modality.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: FGN is a viable sutureless alternative to MN in selected repairs, potentially shortening operative time and minimizing suture-related manipulation. The adjunctive use of platelet-rich plasma may be associated with faster functional recovery without introducing major safety issues. Prospective, randomized studies targeting specific peripheral nerve injuries, such as brachial plexus lesions, are needed. Rigorous control of comorbidities, treatment variables, along with the use of appropriate control groups should be considered to compare different nerve repair modalities and clarify the therapeutic role of bioactive agents in peripheral nerve repair.

sec.title: Type of study/level of evidence
sec.p: Therapeutic II.

sec.title: Registration
sec.p: International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO Registration Number: CRD42023397644).

sec.title: Funding statement
sec.p: This project was completed without any funding.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: Data sharing is not applicable to this article as no new data were created or analyzed in this study.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors have no conflicts of interest.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: The authors wish to acknowledge Larry J. Prokop from Mayo Clinic for his expert opinion on the systematic literature search for this manuscript.

fn.p: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jpra.2026.02.027.
